planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Magnolia liliiflora is the parent species that was crossed with M. denudata in China to produce the famous saucer magnolia (M. x soulangeana), and is itself a beautiful spring-flowering small shrub or tree with deep purple-red to rose-purple flowers.

directions: Plant in spring or fall in moist, slightly acidic, organically rich, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; avoid sites with late spring frosts as the flowers can be damaged in zone 5–6. Magnolia liliiflora is the wild lily magnolia of central and western China, a small, multi-stemmed shrub or small tree (8–12 feet) that produces distinctive, tulip-shaped flowers with deep purple-red to rose-magenta exteriors and white to pale lilac interiors from late March to May (late spring); it is the parent species crossed with M. denudata by Chinese and French horticulturalists to produce the beloved saucer magnolia (M. x soulangeana). The flowers are later-emerging than many magnolias and more reliably miss late freezes in zones 5–6. Grown as an elegant specimen or mixed border plant; 'Nigra' is the most widely grown cultivar, with deeper purple flowers than the species. The species has been cultivated in Chinese gardens for over 1,400 years.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants or divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, moderately fertile soil in partial shade; Clematis versicolor is a native vine of glades, cedar barrens, and rocky limestone woods in the Ozarks and surrounding region, producing urn-shaped, pale lavender to greenish flowers in midsummer followed by showy, feathery seed heads.

directions: Plant at the base of a fence, trellis, or shrub where the roots can be shaded; shade the root zone with a stone or low groundcover while allowing the vine to climb into sunlight. Clematis versicolor (pale leatherflower) is a rare and seldom-cultivated native vine of the Ozarks and Interior Highlands of Missouri, Arkansas, and adjacent states, found on rocky, limestone-derived glades and open cedar-hardwood barrens in thin, alkaline soil; its urn-shaped or pendent, pale lavender to greenish-purple flowers (about 1 inch long) appear in midsummer after most other clematis have finished blooming, followed by large, globe-shaped, feathery seed heads that are attractive in fall. One of the leatherflower clematis group (Viorna section), sharing the nodding, urn-shaped flower form with C. crispa and C. pitcheri. Exceptionally drought-tolerant once established; grows naturally in dry, thin soils over rocky limestone and does not want summer irrigation. An outstanding rare native vine for dry, partially shaded garden settings.

planting.note: Plant grafted bare-root or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Giant Fuyu is the largest-fruiting of the non-astringent Fuyu-type Japanese persimmons, producing fruits that can reach 1 pound each and are eaten crisp like apples when fully colored — no need to wait for softening.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; persimmons are drought-tolerant once established but benefit from consistent moisture during fruit development. Giant Fuyu is a large-fruited, non-astringent Japanese persimmon cultivar, an improved selection from the standard 'Fuyu' producing much larger fruits (often 8–16 oz per fruit, compared to 4–6 oz for standard Fuyu) while retaining the outstanding non-astringent quality of the Fuyu type — meaning the fruits can be eaten firm and crisp right off the tree when fully orange without any astringency, like eating a sweet, spiced apple. An excellent choice for Gulf Coast, California, and mild-climate gardens where it ripens in October–November; trees are self-fruitful, moderate in vigor, and begin producing in 3–5 years from grafted trees. The fruit has beautiful fall color and the bare orange fruits persisting on leafless branches in late fall are ornamentally striking.

common_name: Desert Sage (Mojave Sage, Gray Ball Sage)

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in very well-drained, poor to moderately fertile, alkaline to neutral, dry soil in full sun; Salvia dorrii is native to the Great Basin, Mojave Desert, and adjacent dry mountain ranges, requiring excellent drainage and low moisture — it will decline quickly in heavy, moist soils.

directions: Plant in spring in extremely well-drained, dry, alkaline to neutral, gravelly or sandy soil in full sun; desert sage is intolerant of heavy clay or summer moisture and is best treated as a xeriscape plant that thrives on neglect in dry, arid conditions. Salvia dorrii (desert sage) is a beautiful, fragrant native sage of the Great Basin, Mojave Desert, and interior mountain ranges of the American West, forming compact, rounded shrubs 18–36 inches tall with densely silver-woolly, aromatic foliage and brilliant purple-violet flowers in spring and early summer that are among the most intensely colored of any western native shrub; the strong, camphor-sage fragrance of the foliage is cleansing and memorable, particularly after rain. Extremely cold-hardy for a desert plant (surviving zone 4 conditions) and exceptionally drought-tolerant once established — it evolved in habitats with 6–12 inches of annual rainfall and grows best in similar lean, dry conditions. An outstanding plant for western xeriscape gardens, rock gardens, and dry borders.

planting.note: Start from cuttings or divisions in spring; Origanum onites (pot marjoram or Turkish oregano) is the oregano of eastern Mediterranean and Turkish cuisine, with stronger, more resinous, pungent flavor than sweet marjoram and milder, sweeter flavor than Greek or Italian oregano — used as a bridge herb between the two in Middle Eastern and Turkish cooking.

directions: Propagate from stem cuttings or divisions in spring; plant in very well-drained, lean, rocky to sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun — pot marjoram is a Mediterranean native that dislikes heavy, moist soils and grows best in conditions similar to its native rocky hillside habitats. Origanum onites is distinct from both sweet marjoram (O. majorana) and common Greek oregano (O. vulgare subsp. hirtum), occupying a middle flavor ground between the two: it is more aromatic and peppery than sweet marjoram but less aggressively pungent than the intense Greek species used on pizza. In Turkish and eastern Mediterranean cooking it is the standard 'kekik' (thyme/oregano) used on meat, in köfte, on lahmacun, and in herb blends; the white to pale pink flowers in summer are smaller than many oregano species. Hardy only to about 5–10°F (zone 7); treat as an annual or pot plant in cooler climates.
